title: Data Packing for Microsoft SQL Server
breadcrumbs: Data Packing for Microsoft SQL Server
text: Data Packing for Microsoft SQL Server Uniface packing codes are mapped to MS SQL storage formats (data types). You can specify the way Uniface packing codes are mapped to MS SQL storage formats using the mapping connector option. The default is type 2 mapping. Search: Column Uniface Packing Code MS SQL Storage Format (mapping 2) MS SQL Storage Format (mapping 1) Filter results... 65 rows found Table 1. By default, the MSS U5.0 connector uses the MAX specifier for the Varchar, NVarchar and VarBinary storage formats. The MSS U4.0 connector uses the deprecated text , ntext and image data types. Note: The U5.0 connector can access an existing database in which the U4.0 data types mappings are used. U5.0 differs from U4.0 only when creating tables from entities with segmented fields (SC*, SW*, SR*). Table 2. Default Mappings for Segmented Fields MSS U5.0 and U4.0 Uniface Packing Code U4.0 Mapping U5.0 Mapping SC* Text Varchar(Max) SW* NText NVarchar(Max) SR* Image VarBinary(max) Overflow Tables The following Uniface packing codes require an overflow table when a large amount of data is inserted into the field: C* R* U* W* VC* VR* VU* VW* For more information, see Table and Field Management on Microsoft SQL Server . MS SQL Storage Formats The following table is intended as a guide, not a complete discussion of MS-SQL storage formats. If you are in any doubt about the exact requirements in your environment, see your MS-SQL documentation for more information. Search: Column Storage format Description Filter results... 21 rows found Table 3. MS SQL Storage Formats Storage format Description Filter 21 rows found Filter 21 rows found Storage format Description Binary Use Binary storage format fields for fixed-length binary data, up to a maximum of 255 positions (approximately 2000 bits). Do not specify the binary storage format for hexadecimal data, which should be stored in one of the integer or float formats. Accessing Binary fields in MS-SQL is usually faster than accessing Varbinary fields, but more storage space is used if a Binary field is less than the specified length. Binary fields which allow a NULL value are treated as Varbinary fields if they are NULL. Bit Use the Bit storage format for fields which store TRUE or FALSE values. Bit fields are equivalent to the Uniface Boolean data type. They are called Bit fields because their storage size is one bit and they can contain only the values 0, 1, or NULL. You cannot index a Bit field, so do not use these fields as key fields. Char Char storage formats are used for textual information that has a maximum length of 255 characters with mapping 1. Uniface generates Varchars if the Char storage format is longer than 32 characters, but is capable of handling Chars up to a maximum length of 255 characters with mapping 1. Note: Accessing Char fields in MS-SQL is usually faster than accessing Varchar fields, although more storage space is used if a field is less than the specified length. Be warned, however, that Char fields which allow NULL values are treated like Varchars if their values are NULL. Arithmetic with Char fields is impossible without first converting the data into a numerical format, even if the field contains only numeric data. Date Date variables are stored as three-byte integers that represent dates ranging from January 1 of the year 1 through 31 December, 9999. This data type is used for Date fields in the Uniface model if Mapping Table 23 is used, and it is only applicable MS SQL 2008 and higher. Datetime Datetime storage formats are eight-byte fields, that represent dates ranging from 1 January 1753 through 12 December 9999. The time of day is stored to a precision of 1/300 second. Linear date and time values are all mapped to Datetime fields. When using MS-SQL, Uniface always stores any date or any time in a Datetime format. If no time is available with a date record, 00:00:00 is added by default. If time is recorded without date information, Uniface automatically adds the date that MS-SQL understands as the base date (that is, 01-JAN-1900). Arithmetic functions with Datetime are possible. Note: Beware of handling Uniface Time fields (data type T) with other tools (for example, ISQL), because Uniface maps T fields to Datetime in MS-SQL and adds the base date of 01-JAN-1900. Other tools probably do not do this (ISQL does not), which can cause time values to change inexplicably, and become unintelligible to Uniface. Uniface handles time to a precision of 1/100 second, whereas MS-SQL handles time to a precision of 1/300 second. You will experience problems with time values if Datetime fields are filled by non-Uniface tools which use a greater precision than that of Uniface, and you want to retrieve on those fields with an exact match. For this reason, do not define any primary key, foreign key, or index fields as Datetime. Smalldatetime Smalldatetime fields are less precise than Datetime fields. The storage size is four bytes: one small integer for the number of days after January 1, 1900, and one small integer for the number of minutes since midnight. Dates range from January 1, 1900 to June 6, 2079, with a precision of one minute. Float Float is a storage format for floating point numbers. Positive and negative numbers are allowed, as are numbers with exponential components (which are useful for scientific notation). The storage size is eight bytes. Real This is a four-byte version of the Float storage format. Int Int is a storage format for integers between - 2,147,483,647 and 2,147,483,647 inclusive. The storage size is four bytes. No decimal places or fractions are allowed, but negative numbers are permitted and all arithmetic functions are possible. Smallint Smallint is an integer storage format for whole numbers between -32,768 and 32,767. Negative numbers are therefore allowed. Fractions and decimal places are not allowed. All arithmetic functions are possible. The storage size is two bytes. Tinyint Tinyint is a numerical storage format for integers between zero and 255. Negative numbers, fractions, and decimals are not allowed. All arithmetic functions are possible with Tinyint data. The storage size of Tinyint fields is one byte. Uniface uses this storage format slightly differently to MS-SQL, storing signed values between -128 and 127. This distinction is only important if you access your data with a tool other than Uniface. Image Image fields have the same characteristics as Varbinary fields, but can be of unlimited length (up to the limits imposed upon you by the system). Note that it is not possible to specify Image fields in retrieve profiles, or to search for specific data within the overall contents of the field. Note: Use the image storage format wisely. Storing as little as one byte of data in an image field causes MS-SQL to make a 2 KB NULL storage area. Image fields do not require a storage area of 2 KB. Money Use the Money storage format for any decimal currency values that you need to enter, unless you prefer to use Smallmoney. All arithmetic operations are possible, except modulo. The data entered should not include commas or currency notation. Smallmoney Smallmoney fields can contain values between -214,748.3648 and 214,748.3647 with a precision of 1/10,000 of a unit. All arithmetic operations are possible, except modulo. Numeric Numeric is a numerical storage format for exact numbers between -10 38 and 10 38 -1. Uniface only supports this data type with a precision of up to N32. You need to start up MS-SQL with the ‘ -p ’ flag to use this precision. Text Text fields have the same characteristics as Char fields, but can be of unlimited length (up to the limits imposed upon you by the system). Note: Use the Text storage format wisely. Storing as little as one character in a text field causes MS-SQL to create a NULL storage area of 2 KB. Text fields do not require a storage area of 2 KB. Varchar Use Varchar when a variable-length field is required, up to a maximum length of 255 positions. Varchar fields, like Char fields, can be used for any combination of characters, numbers, and symbols. Use this storage format when the expected length of your data can vary considerably, in a name field for example, when the same field will be used to hold the values ‘JJB Inc.’ and ‘International Sub-Oceanic Refuse Engineering Corporation’. The storage size of a Varchar field depends on its contents, if the field is empty, no space is used. Varbinary Use Varbinary fields for variable-length binary data. Like Binary fields, this storage format is useful for storing programming data, or pictures. The difference lies in the expected length of your data. Use the Varbinary storage format when the length of your data can vary considerably. The storage size of a Varbinary field depends on its contents, if the field is empty, no space is used. Ntext Same as Text, but for Unicode characters. Nchar Same as Char, but for Unicode characters. Nvarchar Same as Varchar, but for Unicode characters. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
